title: Persea subcordata
taxonomy: Persea subcordata is classified within the Lauraceae family. This family is a group of flowering plants commonly known as the laurel family. The Lauraceae family includes approximately 2,000 to 3,000 species worldwide. Persea subcordata is native to the Philippines and is part of the genus Persea. The genus Persea is primarily composed of tropical evergreen trees and shrubs.
uses: Persea subcordata, a plant native to the tropical regions of the Pacific, has been found to possess several medicinal properties. Its bark and leaves have been traditionally used to treat various health issues, including fever, rheumatism, and digestive problems. The plant's extracts have been shown to exhibit anti-inflammatory and antimicrobial activities, making it a potential candidate for the treatment of infections. In traditional medicine, Persea subcordata is also used to treat respiratory issues, such as coughs and colds. However, more research is needed to fully understand the plant's medicinal potential and its effects on human health.

intro: Persea subcordata, also known as Ceylon satinwood or Ceylonese satinwood, is a species of evergreen tree native to Sri Lanka and southern India. The tree is valued for its wood, which is rich in bioactive constituents, including flavonoids, alkaloids, and terpenoids, that have been found to possess anti-inflammatory, antioxidant, and antimicrobial properties, providing potential health benefits such as reduced inflammation and improved cardiovascular health. The leaves, bark, and roots of Persea subcordata are traditionally used in herbal medicine to treat various ailments, including fever, cough, and digestive issues. The most common herbal preparations made with this plant include teas, infusions, and extracts, often used to support immune function and overall well-being.
